utterances: Let's go next to Danny(ph), Danny with us from Lexington, Kentucky.

We're talking with Political Junkie Ken Rudin.

Well, I'll try to keep this under two hours...
Well, I mean, these tapes are pretty devastating.

And, you know, while a lot of the attention of the Blagojevich trials focused on the allegation that he tried to sell the former Senate seat of Barack Obama for personal profit and political profit, there are still a number of - in these 24 counts, there's a number of elements here, regarding the fact that Blagojevich was allegedly trying to shake down the head of a children's hospital in exchange for state funding; to try to shake down Rahm Emanuel's brother to hold a fundraiser in exchange for school funding; and then-Congressman Emanuel's congressional district, to shake down road contractors and basically to see what - how much money they'd give to determine what size of road programs should be done for the Illinois Toll Highway Authority.
There was also a race track that was trying to get back...
Correct.
...in business, and they were being shaken down as well, allegedly.
In exchange for legislation that would be beneficial to help the race - horseracing industry in Illinois.
Let's go next to Jeff(ph), Jeff with us from Superior in Wisconsin.
Yes, good afternoon, gentlemen.
I voted for Rod Blagojevich when I lived in Chicago, and I think he has done a lot for the people in Illinois.
I wish I could vote for him again, actually.
I think we need people that can get results.
And I think he's going to be exonerated.
I think he'll be found not guilty of these crimes.
And it's a new day in Illinois politics, so I just think it's going to be a historic moment.
Have you followed the case closely, Jeff?
Oh, yeah.
It's like an episode in ""Boston Legal."" I can't wait - I would love to see Denny Crane do the closing argument.
I mean, it's great for politics in America.
It's dramatic.
It's exceptional.
We've seen them on Donald Trump's program.
I mean, but I think when it all plays out, I think it's going to be a lesson for all of us that, you know, in order to get results in our government, sometimes, you go to play the political game.
Well...

Unfortunately, some of those results were the fact that the state of Illinois is now - it's tottering on bankruptcy, $13 billion state deficit, over $5 billion in unpaid bills, the least funded public pension system in the United States.
And these were all a result of decisions that basically started under Rod Blagojevich.
Well, there was a lot of black people, I know, that really appreciate the things he did; and a lot of senior citizens who don't have to pay for public transportation.
And they...

And in fact, there's a great tape that was on election night that was recorded by the federal government, filled with expletives, where Blagojevich is lamenting how Barack Obama has now passed him up as a supreme politician from Illinois and complains that he gave her grandmother a free bus ride, I gave your baby health care.
Thirteen percent - which was his job approval rating at the time - and basically told voters to perform an act on themselves.
Jeff, thanks very much for the call.
Appreciate it.
What would happen in the event of an acquittal?
If the governor, if the ex-governor is found not guilty, how does his status change?
Well, I don't think it changes at all.
I mean, the fact is he has been impeached.
It was a unanimous vote of the Illinois State Senate and also a separate vote that barred him from ever seeking public office in Illinois ever again.
That doesn't change.
There is no appeals process for impeachment.
I mean, it's a constitutional standard in the state of Illinois.
I mean, I - certainly, I assume that we'll do something to further enhance his profile for, you know, the various reality shows and his Chicago talk show on radio.
Did the prosecution's evidence include some of the governor's former aides, saying, yes, we were told to do this?

Well, indeed, a lot of the prosecution case was the fact that these were aides that actually became very profitable lobbyists, largely due to the fact of Blagojevich being governor - the insider access that they were allowed.
His former chief of staff, I mean, a college room mate, Alonzo ""Lon"" Monk, was one of those.
John Wyma, who was a former chief of staff when Blagojevich was a congressman, who said he earned a million dollars the first year that Blagojevich became governor.
All these people testifying the fact they wanted to continue to maintain that access, and so that they didn't necessarily questioned some of the harebrained schemes that he was proposing.
Several of them accepting lesser sentences in return for their testimony.
Or just getting a grant of immunity to testify.
Monk, however, his chief of staff, has a plea agreement.
And Rod Blagojevich's brother, how many charges does he face?
He has not faced as many.
As I recall, it was about a handful of charges.
And frankly, most observers' kind of view, if there is a weak part of - in the prosecution of what Rob Blagojevich really will face here.
Well, Rick Pearson, thank you very much for your time today.
We can't wait for the closing arguments on Monday.
They'll be very interesting.
Thanks, Neal.

Well, are they all pol--are there, though, too many politicians and not enough scientists in NOAA?
Well, I wouldn't characterize it quite that way.

I'd been keeping one of the members of one of the families apprised of some conversations that I'd been having with the North Koreans about this issue, and I'd had several extensive chats and exchanges with one of my North Korean counterparts about this matter, and all the while encouraging him and his government to do the right thing.
And as I said, keeping one of the families apprised of my efforts.
And did it seem to you that there was any movement in that direction?
From the very outset of my initial conversation with the North Koreans, I had the clear impression that they were looking for a way to resolve this ultimately.
They were, A, clearly determined to have their legal system do what their legal system ultimately did, which was to investigate, try, convict and sentence these two young ladies.
But at the same time, I think they were looking beyond that for a way to ultimately resolve this issue by allowing the two young ladies to go.

And I think the, sort of, treatment that they provided the two young ladies is an indication that the North Koreans intended all along to have this resolved in the positive fashion that it appears to have been resolved.

Would it be your assumption then that this deal was essentially done ahead of time - in other words, that North Korea wouldn't have invited President Clinton and that President Clinton wouldn't have gone unless it was pretty sure that a deal would be struck to free the journalists?
There have extensive conversations going on in the so-called New York channel between representatives of the Obama administration and the North Koreans.
And those conversations, I am certain, were conversations in which the framework of this visit, and the possible framework of a release, were probably discussed.
Ultimately, the decision was made at the last moment by the North Korean leader.
But I think that the outline of this, including the idea of a U.S.
envoy traveling to North Korea, was probably discussed quite sometime ago, and there were probably a number of names and options that were discussed during the course of that process.

I don't know when the idea of sending former President Clinton was decided upon, but we've all heard lots of names discussed as potential envoys here and I think the choice of President Clinton was a very good one.
When you say that North Korea was looking for a way to resolve this, their motivation would be what and what would they want in exchange?
Hmm, good point, good question.

I think part of the North Korean perception of this issue was their understanding that the longer they held these two young journalists, the more the international community was focused on them, their penal system, their labor camp system and their judicial system and that probably was not a very helpful focus.
I mean, all of us have looked at these stories in recent weeks and they've been very uncomfortable to observe these stories about what goes on inside North Korea: A.

B: I think the North Koreans also realize that the continuing incarceration of these two young ladies in the North probably presented a major burden to them if they were hoping to, as I think they were hoping to, reengage with the United States and the international community in a positive way to try to resolve some of the major issues between us.

And even though this was a separate issue, as everybody has continued to state, as one of your observers stated earlier, everything is political and the North Koreans realize that without the resolution of this issue, the atmospherics would not - could not be created to have a good exchange with the United States.

So, I think by putting this issue behind them, as they are in the process of doing literally as we speak, I think the North Koreans are probably hoping that this may be able to set in process, a process of dialogue with the United States.
